column1: # GENERATED by `python -m puckworks.paper3.evidence_graph --write` from EVIDENCE_LINKS.json + the live registry. DO NOT EDIT BY HAND — edits are overwritten and fail CI. Curate EVIDENCE_LINKS.json instead.

column18: observable
column19: claim
column20: claim_not_supported

column18: Minimum composite porosity (eps_min vs eps0) and the 9-bar Q(t) RMSE vs the measured Waszkiewicz trace (g/s).
column19: Adding the parameter-free mo2023_2 swelling branch behaves as the framework predicts: the composite porosity closes below eps0 and the 9-bar Q(t) residual jumps from ~0.12 to ~0.65 g/s (worse than the flat null ~0.603), diagnosing that mo2023_2's fixed-dP fresh-grain swelling is mis-scaled for an already-swollen saturated pre-wet rig.
column20: Does NOT show swelling improves or explains the 9-bar flow — the opposite: passing certifies the swelling branch makes the prediction worse and must not be tuned to hide the residual.

column18: Mass flow Q(t) at 9 bar vs the measured Waszkiewicz trace over the 15-95 s saturated window (RMSE, g/s).
column19: In that extraction-only reduction the coupled model's predicted 9-bar Q(t) reproduces the measured Waszkiewicz 9-bar flow ramp with RMSE ~0.113 g/s (gate <0.13), matching the poroelastic component's own reconstruction (rung 4).
column20: A passing RMSE is NOT independent validation of the kappa(t) mechanism; it is the same-rig reconstruction the poroelastic component already achieved, inherited unchanged.

column18: Structural identity of the coupled model's extraction-only Phi(t)/Q(t) closure to the standalone poroelastic component (a code property).
column19: With the swelling/compaction/fines-loss branches disabled, the coupled shared-porosity synthesis reduces exactly to its degenerate special case waszkiewicz2025.poroelastic (extraction-only Phi(t)=m_d(t)/m0 through the same poroelastic closure), adding no new behaviour in this limit.
column20: Does NOT validate the full four-branch synthesis nor show the poroelastic physics is independently correct — only that the two models are indistinguishable when losses are off.

column18: Darcy permeability k of a plane-Poiseuille channel in lattice units vs the closed-form analytic value h^2/12.
column19: The D3Q19 TRT lattice-Boltzmann permeability kernel reproduces the exact plane-Poiseuille channel permeability h^2/12 to within 0.5% (reported +0.03%).
column20: Validates solver numerics against an analytic channel only — not against coffee/permeability data or sphere-drag at production resolution.

column18: Extraction yield EY (%) via two internal accounting routes plus an inventory upper bound, all computed from the simulation and module constants.
column19: The cameron2020 BDF two-population solver conserves solute mass (cup-mass EY agrees with solid-depletion EY to within 0.5 pp) and the predicted EY stays strictly below the model's own per-bed soluble-inventory ceiling.
column20: Does NOT establish that the model's EY matches any measured espresso yield — it is a numerical mass-budget self-consistency check, not empirical validation.

column18: First-liquid / bed-saturation time (s): observed = time cup weight first exceeds 0.5 g; predicted = t_saturate when the modelled front reaches bed depth L, at porosity endpoints 0.173 and 0.322.
column19: A sharp-front infiltration prediction, driven by the DE1 shot's own measured pressure trace and a permeability from that shot's fitted kappa, yields a bed-saturation-time window (across an a-priori porosity bracket 0.173-0.322) that brackets the observed first-drip time (~7 s).
column20: Does NOT demonstrate a parameter-free prediction independent of the evaluated data: k and P(t) are from the same shot, so 'parameter-free' holds only in that no parameter was tuned to hit the first-drip time itself.

column18: Normalized bed flow Q/Qm = min(Q_p, f(H,s))/Q_m vs experiment time.
column19: The re-implemented Foster machine-mode ODE (pump characteristic + trapped-air headspace) reproduces the paper's own Fig 15 normalized bed-flow curve, dipping to Q/Qm~0.181 at t~2.0 s and recovering, to RMSE <0.01 over the t_p..t_s window.
column20: It does not validate the mid-shot flow minimum against any measured flow trace, nor establish that pump+headspace dynamics cause real early-shot flow dips (CT validation is a separate gate).

column18: Two scalar times (s): ponding time t_p and saturation time t_s, model-computed vs the authors' reported values.
column19: The staged pump/headspace machine-mode ODE, run from the transcribed Table I/II parameters and offset by the fitted t_shift=0.796 s, reproduces the authors' reported ponding time t_p=0.823 s and saturation time t_s=6.669 s.
column20: Not a test against independent experimental first-drip/CT timing — it reproduces the paper's own reported model-output numbers from the paper's own fitted parameters.

column18: The dimensionless equilibrium desorption product K*E_max from equilibrium TDS vs brew ratio R_brew for full-immersion brewing.
column19: Re-fitting the lumped equilibrium product K*E_max via Eq. 11 to the digitized Fig. 3 TDS-vs-brew-ratio data (R_brew>=3) recovers ~0.215, the card's reported value.
column20: Does NOT validate the desorption model against independent data or for espresso/flow — only that the re-estimate reproduces the paper's reported value from the same curve.

column18: Fixed-dp flow-decay ratio q(60s)/q(0) (dp/mu/L cancelled) per powder E/H/M/F, plus the scalar s_m.
column19: The re-implemented swelling-PDE -> Eq.21 bed-porosity -> Carman-Kozeny chain reproduces the paper's SIMULATED Fig 3(a) fixed-dp flow-decay ratio q(60s)/q(0) for all four powders within 20%, preserves the coarser-throttles-less ordering E<H<M<F, and matches the closed-form max swelling s_m=3.57%.
column20: It does not test the swelling-throttled-flow mechanism against any measured flow-decay data; the ordering is a reproduced property of the source's model, not an experimentally confirmed fact.

column18: End-of-shot yield (fraction of extractable inventory) with swelling on vs off at q=2/3/4 mL/s (fixed-q), contrasted with the fixed-dP Carman-Kozeny flow-decay ratio.
column19: At fixed flow the implemented mo2023_2 two-population model gives nearly identical yield with swelling on vs off (max relative difference <5%) because swelling raises grain porosity/D_p (~+15%) but grows R^2 (~+7%) and these offset — reproducing the paper's Fig. 2 model behaviour.
column20: Does NOT validate the swelling mechanism or absolute yields against experiment — only that the code exhibits the offsetting-timescale insensitivity the paper asserts at fixed flow.

column18: Per-solute MAPE (%) between simulated and measured fraction concentrations over 15 experiments x 6 time-fraction windows.
column19: Run with the authors' Table 2 per-solute fitted parameters, the two-grain multi-solute PDE solver reproduces the authors' own published fit MAPEs against the Schmieder kinetics (TDS 6.07, caffeine 4.59, trigonelline 7.85, CGA 4.98 %, each within ~3.5 pts).
column20: Does NOT demonstrate predictive accuracy on any independent/out-of-sample data — fit and evaluation are the same data.

column18: Mean percentage error (MPE, %) between the thesis's predicted and measured espresso yields per molecular-weight Deff class, digitized from Fig. 7.4 (~+/-0.5 pp).
column19: Across all 15 digitized Fig. 7.4 espresso conditions the thesis's reported medium-MW (non-fitted microstructural Deff) bed-scale prediction error is MPE <=14.3%, and the medium-MW single-Deff column has a lower mean MPE than the low- or high-MW columns.
column20: Does NOT reproduce the yields from any puckworks solver and does NOT test an independent lab/campaign — all data are from the same thesis and apparatus family.

column18: Per-cell percent offset (mu_khomyakov - mu_TR2001)/mu_TR2001 over the overlapping concentration/temperature cells, summarized by median/min/max and sign.
column19: Two independent measured coffee-extract viscosity datasets (khomyakov2020 kinematic converted to dynamic mu=rho*nu vs telisromero2001 Eq-10 closure) agree in sign/direction across their Xw 76-90% / T 295-365 K overlap but khomyakov runs systematically ~+37% (median 25-50%, max <60%) above TR2001.
column20: Does NOT establish which source is correct nor validate either at espresso TDS — only records a bounded, systematic inter-source magnitude discrepancy over the measured overlap box.

column18: Reference espresso dynamic-viscosity ratio to pure water at brew temperature, plus Newtonian-band membership.
column19: The transcribed reference espresso viscosity estimate (~4-6e-4 Pa.s, ~1.3-1.9x pure water 3.15e-4 Pa.s) exceeds pure water, stays within 1.0-2.5x, and lies in the Newtonian band (espresso 4-12% solids is below the ~36%-solids power-law onset).
column20: Does NOT measure or validate espresso-liquor viscosity — only that a transcribed, extrapolated reference value is above water and within a Newtonian bound.

column18: Density rho, specific heat cp, thermal conductivity k and thermal diffusivity alpha of soluble-coffee extract vs T and water fraction (plus a unit guard).
column19: The transcribed Telis-Romero 2000 linear thermophysical closures (rho/cp/k/alpha) reproduce the card's figure-endpoint / water-limit anchors within plotting accuracy, the typo-corrected Eq(6) alpha coefficient reproduces the Fig-5 endpoint while the printed one does not, and the fraction-vs-percent guard raises on percent input.
column20: It does NOT independently validate coffee-extract thermophysical properties (no external measurement) and says nothing about viscosity or flow — it confirms faithful transcription and flags the Eq-6 typo.

column18: Newtonian dynamic viscosity eta (Pa.s) and power-law consistency index K (Pa.s^n) of soluble-coffee extract vs temperature and water fraction.
column19: The independently transcribed Telis-Romero 2001 Eq(10)/(13) viscosity closures reproduce the full digitized Table 1 (24 eta cells) and Table 2 (27 K cells) at about the authors' own reported fit error, cross-checking the digitization against the transcribed coefficients.
column20: It does NOT validate espresso-liquor viscosity nor independently confirm the closures against another lab/coffee — it only shows the two transcription paths of the SAME paper are mutually consistent.

column18: Darcy flow-suppression factor (mu_water / mu_local) implied by concentration-dependent liquor viscosity over a simulated shot's in-pore concentration field.
column19: Driving cameron2020's own in-pore liquor concentration field through the MEASURED Telis-Romero 2001 eta(c,T) grid, the in-pore liquor never approaches saturation (peak mu < 1.15x water), so the constant-water-viscosity assumption used by every registered flow model gives <8% outlet flow suppression and >0.95 depth-averaged flow factor at espresso conditions.
column20: It does NOT empirically demonstrate real espresso flow is insensitive to liquor viscosity nor validate cameron2020's concentration field — only that IF that field is right, the measured mu(c,T) correction is small.

column18: Pump/basket pressure drop dP (bar) vs flow Q over the working range, bounded against manufacturer free-flow debit and deadhead endpoints.
column19: The operative measured single-rig Waszkiewicz brewer quadratic (dP=aQ^2+bQ+c) stays inside the Ulka manufacturer envelope over the working flow band (dP below the 15-bar deadhead, free-flow > 1.90 g/s) — a bounds consistency check between two on-disk datasets.
column20: It does NOT establish the quadratic is the correct pump physics or holds outside the measured band (the true vibe-pump curve is concave), nor provide an independent DE1-specific characteristic.

column18: Peak Forchheimer number Fo_F = rho*k*q/(mu*k_I) evaluated along the recorded trace.
column19: Computing Fo_F along the real DE1 fixture-A tamped trace (Darcy k from the fitted kappa=1.196 via the kappa->SI chain, k_I from the ceramics-fitted Zhou closure) yields a peak Fo_F that is O(1), falls inside the backlog's 0.3-0.9 gusher-regime estimate, and exceeds the card's untamped espresso ceiling (0.0639) by >10x.
column20: It does not empirically validate that espresso flow is inertial nor measure Fo_F; only that the model's computed Fo_F on one uncontrolled community fixture is compatible with the backlog's higher estimate.

column18: Dimensionless collapse ratio k_measured / (k_star(phi_p,s_p)*phi_p^4.4) across the Table 1 grind rows, aggregated as a geometric mean.
column19: The paper's percolation permeability form k = k_star(phi_p,s_p)*phi_p^4.4 collapses their own Table 1 measured permeabilities to a geometric-mean ratio near 0.9 (gate window 0.7 < GM < 1.2).
column20: Does NOT independently validate k(R,phi) against out-of-sample or tamped-bed permeability data.

column18: Mass flow Q(t) at 9 bar vs time (long-run mean g/s and Pearson correlation, t>=15 s).
column19: With zero extra free parameters (static (P_c,Q_c) plus the dissolution sigmoid Phi(t)=m_d(t)/m0), Eq. 18 reproduces the measured 9-bar Q(t) ramp semi-quantitatively — long-run mean within 10% and correlation >0.90 over t>=15 s.
column20: A passing correlation does NOT show the mechanism is predictive on a new pressure/rig/coffee, nor that dissolution (rather than swelling, per the authors' own CT) drives the change.

column18: Steady-state basket-pressure vs mass-flow curve; recovered calibration parameters P_c, Q_c.
column19: Refitting the poroelastic equilibrium closure (Eq. 16) to the 11-pressure long-run pressure-flow curve recovers the calibration pair (P_c,Q_c) ~ (12 bar, 1.90 g/s) and reproduces the paper's ingested static calibration to <0.05 bar / <0.01 g/s.
column20: Does NOT independently validate the poroelastic model or transfer to another rig/coffee/grind — only confirms the refit reproduces the authors' own calibration on their own data.
